nativeRange.habitat: The Axilspot Hogfish inhabits clear lagoon waters and seaward reefs across the Indo-Pacific. Adults are most often found at depths of 1 to 8 m, though the species has been recorded as deep as 100 m. Juveniles tend to shelter alone inside caves and under rocky overhangs.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on benthic hard-shelled invertebrates such as molluscs, small crustaceans, and worms. In captivity has been observed to accept a varied carnivore diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped clam or shrimp, and quality marine flakes/pellets. Juveniles have been documented picking parasites from larger fishes in the wild.

summary: The Axilspot Hogfish is a mid-sized Indo-Pacific reef fish that reaches around 20 cm as an adult. Juveniles are boldly patterned in black and white; adults develop a reddish front half with a pale rear and a dark spot at the pectoral-fin base. Generally considered reef-safe with caution.
careNotes:
The Axilspot Hogfish is generally considered an intermediate-level marine fish. Juveniles are striking — black with rows of white spots — and have been documented acting as cleaners, picking parasites off larger fishes on the reef. Adults develop a reddish anterior and white posterior with a distinctive dark blotch at the pectoral-fin base (the 'axil spot'), and tend to be more assertive than juveniles.

Reef compatibility is best described as reef-safe with caution. Adults have been known to prey on small ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, tubeworms, and small bivalves; corals are not typically targeted. If kept in a reef system, introducing the hogfish last and avoiding small motile invertebrates afterward is generally advised.

This is an active species that appreciates open swimming room and a long tank footprint. A minimum of 400 L with a tank length of roughly 150 cm is generally recommended, and larger aquariums are preferable for full-grown adults.

Like most wrasses, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite. Hogfishes are also commonly reported to jump, so a well-fitted lid is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pair formation during spawning. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, individuals typically begin life as females, with dominant individuals transitioning to male. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species detail page lists: 8 in adult, 70 gal minimum, 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, peaceful (slightly territorial with conspecifics), reef-safe with caution, carnivore, moderate care level, Indo-Pacific.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max body length (20 cm SL), taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to French Polynesia), depth range (2–100 m), preferred temperature (24.4–28.9 °C, mean 27.5 °C), diet (hard-shelled invertebrates), juvenile cleaning behaviour, oviparous spawning with distinct pairing, and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2008).

additional.name: Reefapp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20 cm), minimum tank size (~400 L), temperature (~26 °C), salinity (~1.026), and reef-with-caution rating. Notes the species 'revels in swimming' — used to bias the chosen tank volume toward the larger end of the cross-source spread.

additional.name: Reef2Reef
additional.notes: Used as an additional husbandry opinion via web-search summary. Suggests a 125 gal (~473 L) minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, and known jumper (justifies tight lid).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums. In the wild, spawning is oviparous with distinct pair formation, and the species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — individuals typically begin life as female, with dominant fish later transitioning to male.
